refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation communication integrity within customer-defined packet loss limits during agreed network conditions.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) supporting teleoperation as a capability, and (2) meeting a specific quality metric (packet loss). The refined FR isolates the quality/performance obligation while remaining solution-neutral. It avoids prescribing 'how' (e.g., redundancy, error correction, protocol selection) and focuses on the measurable outcome in the problem domain. The phrase 'communication integrity' is more precise than 'communication quality' and encompasses both packet loss and related reliability concerns.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Conduct teleoperation trials under agreed network conditions (e.g., 4G/5G coverage, latency profiles, interference scenarios). Measure end-to-end packet loss rate across representative mission durations. Confirm measured packet loss ≤ customer-defined threshold (e.g., ≤1%). Document network conditions, test duration, payload types, and loss statistics.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The NGHE solution shall enable remote operation of equipment from a tele-operator station.
FR-NGHE-009.2: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation command and feedback data transmission with packet loss ≤ customer-defined limit under agreed network conditions.
FR-NGHE-009.3: The NGHE solution shall detect and report communication degradation (packet loss, latency anomalies) to the tele-operator and safety manager.

candidate_dps: DP1: Implement adaptive packet redundancy and selective retransmission based on real-time network quality metrics (loss rate, latency variance) to maintain command/feedback integrity within customer-defined thresholds.
DP2: Establish a dual-channel communication architecture with independent network paths and automatic failover to preserve teleoperation continuity when primary channel degrades.
DP3: Deploy application-level error detection and correction (FEC or ARQ) with configurable aggressiveness tuned to customer network conditions and acceptable latency bounds.
DP4: Implement predictive network monitoring with proactive buffer management and command queuing to absorb transient packet loss without operator intervention.
DP5: Establish a hybrid approach combining lightweight heartbeat/acknowledgment protocol with selective frame prioritization (critical commands vs. telemetry) to optimize throughput under loss.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP5: Establish a hybrid approach combining lightweight heartbeat/acknowledgment protocol with selective frame prioritization (critical commands vs. telemetry) to optimize throughput under loss.

potential_sources: Coupling between frame prioritization logic and command/feedback message definitions—mitigated by defining priority tiers as abstract functional contracts, not tied to specific message formats.
Coupling between heartbeat interval and network latency assumptions—mitigated by making heartbeat timing configurable per customer network profile.
Coupling between acknowledgment strategy and operator UI responsiveness—mitigated by separating ACK logic (DP) from UI rendering (separate FR/DP pair).
coupling_check.notes: DP5 avoids hard dependencies on specific redundancy technologies (e.g., dual-channel hardware) while remaining solution-neutral. It decouples the communication integrity concern from transport-layer specifics, allowing implementation flexibility (software-only, hardware-assisted, or hybrid). Prioritization and heartbeat are configurable parameters, not fixed design choices.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Customer network conditions are measurable and stable enough to establish baseline packet loss profile. Risk: If network is highly variable or unmeasured, tuning parameters may be ineffective. Mitigation: Require network characterization as part of acceptance testing.

Assumption: Operator can tolerate brief command latency increases during loss events. Risk: Safety-critical commands (e-stop, emergency halt) may be delayed. Mitigation: Define a separate, higher-priority channel or out-of-band mechanism for safety-critical commands.

Assumption: Heartbeat and ACK overhead does not exceed available bandwidth. Risk: On severely constrained links, protocol overhead itself may worsen loss. Mitigation: Make heartbeat interval and ACK batching adaptive; validate bandwidth budget in network profile.

Assumption: Frame prioritization logic can be implemented without introducing race conditions or state inconsistency. Risk: Selective retransmission may cause out-of-order delivery of critical state updates. Mitigation: Enforce sequence numbering and idempotency checks at application layer.

Assumption: Customer-defined packet loss target (≤1%) is achievable under agreed conditions. Risk: If network is inherently worse, no DP can guarantee compliance. Mitigation: Establish clear acceptance test protocol and fallback operational constraints (e.g., reduced speed, shorter range).
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR (refined): The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation communication integrity by detecting and recovering from packet loss within customer-defined limits (target ≤1%) under agreed network conditions, while ensuring safety-critical commands (e-stop, emergency halt) are delivered with higher assurance than routine telemetry. DP5 should be paired with a separate FR/DP for out-of-band safety signaling to avoid coupling safety guarantees to the primary communication protocol. Additionally, define explicit acceptance criteria: (1) network characterization protocol, (2) packet loss measurement methodology, (3) latency bounds for command delivery, and (4) fallback operational modes if network conditions degrade beyond agreed thresholds.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Manages real-time command/telemetry exchange with hybrid heartbeat/acknowledgment protocol and frame prioritization logic for remote vehicle control under lossy network conditions.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives high-priority command directives (critical commands) that must be prioritized in frame scheduling.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Outputs vehicle state telemetry with selective transmission based on frame prioritization (lower priority, subject to loss optimization).

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional interface for heartbeat/acknowledgment protocol exchange and frame transmission under lossy conditions.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Escalates critical safety events that override normal frame prioritization if heartbeat/acknowledgment fails.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit: Teleoperation owns command/telemetry exchange, network protocol optimization, and frame prioritization logic. Heartbeat/acknowledgment is a core teleoperation concern. Minor diffusion: SafetyAlert escalation requires coordination with Safety Services, but decision logic remains in Teleoperation.

Poor fit: Vehicle module owns vehicle state and actuation, not network protocol or command scheduling. Hybrid protocol and frame prioritization are teleoperation concerns, not vehicle-level. High diffusion: protocol logic would leak into vehicle module inappropriately.

Weak fit: Safety Services owns safety validation and alerts, not network optimization. Frame prioritization and heartbeat protocol are operational concerns, not safety-critical validation. Diffusion: protocol logic would scatter across Safety Services and Teleoperation.

Very poor fit: NGHE Solution is a top-level system container, not an implementation module. Placing protocol/prioritization logic here violates separation of concerns and creates overly broad ownership. High diffusion: logic would be scattered across multiple subsystems.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the natural owner of DP5 because it is responsible for managing the bidirectional exchange of commands and telemetry over a network link. The hybrid heartbeat/acknowledgment protocol and selective frame prioritization are core operational mechanisms for optimizing throughput under loss—both are teleoperation concerns, not vehicle dynamics, safety validation, or system-level orchestration. The module has direct access to Tasking (command input), Telemetry (state output), and Network Link (transmission medium), enabling cohesive implementation of protocol logic and frame scheduling without diffusion.
containment_risks: SafetyAlert escalation path: If heartbeat failure triggers safety-critical actions, coordination between Teleoperation and Safety Services must be explicit via SafetyAlert interface to avoid hidden coupling.

Frame prioritization policy: If safety-critical commands are mixed with operational commands in Tasking, Teleoperation must defer to Safety Services for priority override logic; this boundary must be clearly defined.

Network Link abstraction: If Network Link interface does not expose loss/latency metrics, Teleoperation cannot adapt frame prioritization dynamically; ensure Network Link provides sufficient observability.

Telemetry filtering: Selective telemetry transmission (lower-priority frames dropped under loss) must not violate safety or diagnostic requirements; Safety Services must validate acceptable telemetry loss thresholds.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The FR specifies a measurable acceptance criterion (packet loss ≤ 1% or customer-defined target under agreed network conditions). The Teleoperation IM boundary owns the hybrid heartbeat/acknowledgment protocol and frame prioritization logic, enabling direct instrumentation of packet loss metrics, acknowledgment rates, and frame delivery success. Verification can be conducted via network simulation, field testing under controlled loss conditions, and telemetry analysis of protocol performance. The SafetyAlert escalation path provides an observable signal for heartbeat failure events.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. The FR is allocated to the Teleoperation IM with explicit ownership of protocol optimization and frame scheduling. The allocation is traceable: FR → DP5 (hybrid protocol + frame prioritization) → Teleoperation module (owns command/telemetry exchange, network link, and scheduling logic). External interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, Network Link, SafetyAlert) define the module's responsibilities and dependencies without ambiguity.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP5 directly addresses the FR requirement. The hybrid heartbeat/acknowledgment protocol ensures communication integrity by detecting and recovering from packet loss; selective frame prioritization optimizes throughput by protecting critical commands while accepting loss of lower-priority telemetry. The DP is solution-agnostic (does not prescribe specific protocol details) and is implementable within the Teleoperation module. The DP is measurable against the acceptance criterion (packet loss ≤ 1%) and is feasible under agreed network conditions.

diffusion_risks: SafetyAlert escalation coupling: Teleoperation must not embed safety validation logic; if heartbeat failure triggers safety-critical actions, the decision to escalate must be owned by Safety Services. Teleoperation should only signal heartbeat failure via SafetyAlert interface; Safety Services determines if escalation is warranted. Recommend explicit contract: Teleoperation detects and reports heartbeat loss; Safety Services validates and acts.

Frame prioritization policy ownership: If frame priority rules are driven by safety constraints (e.g., 'critical commands always transmitted, telemetry dropped under loss'), this policy must be defined by Safety Services or Tasking domain, not embedded in Teleoperation. Teleoperation should implement a configurable prioritization scheme; policy should be injected from external authority. Risk: Teleoperation becomes a policy engine rather than a protocol executor.

Network Link observability: DP5 requires dynamic adaptation of frame prioritization based on observed loss/latency. If Network Link interface does not expose real-time loss metrics, latency estimates, or link quality signals, Teleoperation cannot implement adaptive prioritization. Ensure Network Link provides sufficient observability (e.g., loss rate, round-trip time, queue depth) to enable DP5 realization.

Telemetry filtering validation: Selective transmission of lower-priority telemetry frames under loss may violate diagnostic or safety requirements (e.g., vehicle health monitoring, audit trails). Teleoperation must not unilaterally decide which telemetry is droppable. Recommend: Safety Services and Tasking define acceptable telemetry loss thresholds; Teleoperation enforces them.

Protocol state management: Heartbeat/acknowledgment protocol maintains state (pending acks, timeout counters, loss history). If this state is scattered across Teleoperation and Network Link (e.g., Network Link manages timeouts, Teleoperation manages retries), diffusion occurs. Ensure protocol state machine is cohesively owned by Teleoperation; Network Link is a passive transmission medium.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Excellent containment (0.92 score). Teleoperation is the natural owner of DP5 because it owns the command/telemetry exchange, has direct access to Tasking (command input), Telemetry (state output), and Network Link (transmission medium), and is responsible for optimizing throughput under loss. The module boundary is well-defined: Teleoperation implements protocol logic and frame scheduling; external modules (Safety Services, Tasking) provide policy and constraints via interfaces. Minor diffusion risk is SafetyAlert escalation, which requires explicit coordination but does not violate module ownership (Teleoperation detects, Safety Services decides). Recommend clarifying the SafetyAlert contract and ensuring Network Link provides sufficient observability for adaptive prioritization.

recommended_revisions: Clarify SafetyAlert escalation contract: Define whether Teleoperation escalates heartbeat failure automatically or only when loss exceeds a threshold. Recommend: Teleoperation reports heartbeat loss event via SafetyAlert; Safety Services determines if operational safety is compromised and initiates failsafe actions. This avoids embedding safety logic in Teleoperation.

Externalize frame prioritization policy: Define frame priority rules (critical commands vs. telemetry) outside Teleoperation. Recommend: Tasking or Safety Services specifies priority levels; Teleoperation implements a configurable scheduler that respects those levels. This decouples protocol implementation from policy.

Enhance Network Link interface specification: Ensure Network Link exposes loss metrics, latency estimates, and link quality signals (e.g., loss rate, round-trip time, queue depth) to enable Teleoperation to adapt frame prioritization dynamically. Recommend: Add observability contract to Network Link interface (e.g., 'Network Link shall report loss rate every 100ms' or 'Network Link shall expose current queue depth').

Validate telemetry loss thresholds: Conduct a safety and diagnostic impact analysis to determine which telemetry frames can be dropped under loss without violating safety or operational requirements. Recommend: Safety Services and Tasking jointly define acceptable telemetry loss thresholds; Teleoperation enforces them via frame prioritization logic.

Consolidate protocol state management: Ensure heartbeat/acknowledgment protocol state (pending acks, timeout counters, loss history) is owned entirely by Teleoperation. Network Link should be a passive transmission medium; Teleoperation manages all protocol state and retries. Recommend: Add state machine specification to Teleoperation IM documentation.

Add acceptance test criteria for DP5: Specify measurable tests for packet loss ≤ 1% under agreed network conditions. Recommend: (1) Simulate packet loss at 1-5% and verify protocol maintains ≤ 1% effective loss via retries; (2) Measure frame delivery latency for critical commands vs. telemetry; (3) Verify SafetyAlert escalation triggers when heartbeat loss exceeds threshold; (4) Validate telemetry filtering does not drop safety-critical frames.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-IM, FR-DP, DP-IM) pass with high confidence. The FR is measurable, the DP is solution-agnostic and directly addresses the FR, and the IM allocation is clear with excellent containment (0.92 score). However, the diffusion risks and recommended revisions reveal that DP5 (hybrid heartbeat/acknowledgment protocol + frame prioritization) contains multiple sub-concerns that require explicit decomposition to avoid embedding policy, safety logic, and observability requirements into the Teleoperation module. The high safety criticality of this system demands that we decompose DP5 into lower-level FRs to clarify ownership boundaries, policy injection points, and state management responsibilities before implementation. Proceeding to the next level will surface and resolve the five key diffusion risks: (1) SafetyAlert escalation contract, (2) frame prioritization policy ownership, (3) Network Link observability, (4) telemetry loss validation, and (5) protocol state cohesion.
next_level_sub_frs: FR5.1: Detect and report heartbeat loss events to Safety Services without embedding safety validation logic or failsafe decisions.

FR5.2: Implement configurable frame prioritization that respects externally-defined priority policies (critical commands vs. telemetry) and adapts dynamically based on observed network loss and latency.
FR5.3: Obtain real-time network link quality metrics (loss rate, latency, queue depth) from Network Link module to enable adaptive frame scheduling.
FR5.4: Enforce externally-defined telemetry loss thresholds to ensure that frame filtering under loss does not violate safety or diagnostic requirements.
FR5.5: Maintain coherent heartbeat/acknowledgment protocol state (pending acks, timeout counters, loss history) within Teleoperation module without diffusion to Network Link.

revise.why: DP5 is correct in concept but requires refinement to explicitly separate concerns: (1) protocol detection and reporting (DP5.1), (2) configurable policy-driven scheduling (DP5.2), (3) observability consumption (DP5.3), (4) threshold enforcement (DP5.4), and (5) state machine ownership (DP5.5). The current DP5 statement conflates these concerns. Revise DP5 to state: 'Implement a hybrid heartbeat/acknowledgment protocol with configurable frame prioritization that (a) detects heartbeat loss and escalates via SafetyAlert interface without embedding safety logic, (b) respects externally-injected priority policies, (c) consumes Network Link observability metrics to adapt scheduling, (d) enforces telemetry loss thresholds defined by Safety Services and Tasking, and (e) maintains protocol state cohesively within Teleoperation.' This revision clarifies the decomposition path and ensures sub-FRs address each diffusion risk.

assumptions: Safety Services module exists and owns safety validation logic; Teleoperation will signal heartbeat loss but not decide failsafe actions.
Tasking or Safety Services will define frame priority rules and telemetry loss thresholds; Teleoperation will implement a configurable scheduler that respects these policies.
Network Link module will expose real-time loss metrics, latency estimates, and link quality signals via a well-defined observability interface.
Telemetry filtering under loss is acceptable provided it does not violate safety-critical or diagnostic requirements; Safety Services and Tasking will jointly validate acceptable loss thresholds.
Heartbeat/acknowledgment protocol state machine is owned entirely by Teleoperation; Network Link is a passive transmission medium without protocol state responsibility.
open_risks: SafetyAlert escalation contract ambiguity: If Teleoperation automatically escalates heartbeat loss without threshold or Safety Services validation, safety logic may be embedded in Teleoperation. Mitigation: Decompose FR5.1 to clarify escalation trigger and ownership.

Frame prioritization policy diffusion: If Teleoperation embeds priority rules (e.g., 'critical commands always transmitted, telemetry dropped'), policy ownership is unclear and changes require code modification. Mitigation: Decompose FR5.2 to enforce external policy injection.

Network Link observability gap: If Network Link does not expose loss metrics or latency estimates, Teleoperation cannot implement adaptive prioritization. Mitigation: Decompose FR5.3 to specify observability contract and validate Network Link capability.

Telemetry loss validation gap: If Teleoperation unilaterally decides which telemetry is droppable, diagnostic or safety requirements may be violated. Mitigation: Decompose FR5.4 to enforce externally-defined thresholds.

Protocol state diffusion: If heartbeat/acknowledgment state is scattered across Teleoperation and Network Link, protocol logic becomes fragmented and difficult to verify. Mitigation: Decompose FR5.5 to consolidate state ownership in Teleoperation.
evidence_needed: Specification of SafetyAlert interface contract: Define whether Teleoperation escalates heartbeat loss automatically, conditionally (e.g., loss > threshold), or only upon Safety Services request. Provide examples of escalation scenarios.
Frame priority policy specification: Document how priority rules are defined, communicated to Teleoperation, and updated at runtime. Provide examples of critical vs. non-critical frames.

Network Link observability specification: Define which metrics (loss rate, latency, queue depth) are exposed, at what frequency, and with what accuracy. Provide examples of how Teleoperation uses these metrics to adapt scheduling.

Telemetry loss impact analysis: Conduct safety and diagnostic impact analysis to identify which telemetry frames can be dropped without violating requirements. Document acceptable loss thresholds for each frame type.
Protocol state machine specification: Document heartbeat/acknowledgment state machine (states, transitions, timeout logic, retry logic) and verify it is owned entirely by Teleoperation.
Acceptance test plan for DP5: Specify measurable tests for packet loss ≤ 1% under agreed network conditions, including simulation, field testing, and telemetry analysis.
